author: @easy_s3351
text:
If calling an animal by its name makes me a purist then so be it. i don't think a fan placed in front of an open window or the extraction fan in a bathroom should be called an air conditioner (they do both create more comfort by moving air) but that's cool, we can agree to disagree on that. Let's not worry too much about what your unit should or shouldn't be called (right now it's becoming a fan coil unit), it's turning in to a pretty cool thing and a rose would still smell like a rose...
BTW, you also have absorption heat pumps that work by pumping a NH3/water solution around using a fluid pump so you don't need to have a compressor to have a heat pump (AC). It's just that the most commonly used heat pumps (air-to-air) have a compressor which is why people think all heat pumps have a compressor.
If you're going to use cold tap water in the heat exchanger you'd effectively be warming up that cold tap water. If you could then store that in an insulated tank you could then use that pre-heated tap water to make warm tap water. If you could get from say 15C to 25C you'd then only have to (gas)heat that 25C water to 45C to have warm tap water. That saves you 1/3 compared to having to (gas)heat from 15C to 45C. Could be interesting.
I think you'd have to move a bit more air to have an actual effect on the room you're trying to condition (;-)), 30 l/min=0,18 m3/h isn't a lot to work with. Maybe a bigger bellow or a couple of them working together, like the double bellows?

Another way really interesting to refresh air is the friction of the air flow with small water falling
If a small pump maintains a small water falling on a curtain of wool thread the air flow pathing through is immediately refresh
This is, i think, the most simple and cheapest way to get constant fresh air
This also give you a rehydration of the ambient air and capture all the small particules of dust witch is also important for health
Air flow water fall fresh air ;-)

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
You can also use the 'pet door' swinging door flapper valve as well.

Just make the doorway of really light foam, mount it to the port with a hinge [or 2 if they are really small] so that the valve is held closed lightly by gravity, and easily pushed open by pressure.

Even a light weight plastic ball or even a balloon [but balloons can shrink over time, or burst, so not the best idea] can be rested over a nice round hole and air would go past under pressure, but the round object would fit over the hole enough to stop back-flow, no hinges, no springs, just make sure it does not pop off and roll away, you can put a small plastic basket, or wire basket, or even a light weight tube to guide it so it can not roll away, in fact the tube can be part of the duct work, just point it up, and you need not do anything else, and on the inlet side, just have the ball/balloon inside the body of the valve so it is facing the right direction of air flow and you're done!

Basically, 2 balls/balloons, a box with 2 holes in it for the valves, and 1 hole for the bellows, and you are done!

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text:
Just some thoughts and trivial information, cold air is heavy and dense compared to hot air therefore I think if you were to pump it directly from the ground then I think a bellows would be best placed in the ground to actually push it rather than try to suck it. I would be careful about taking air out of the ground though as you don't know what gasses could be in the ground. Much safer to use the heat exchanger like you are doing.
evaporative cooling is effective when the relative humidity is low but when it's high like it can be here in Sydney it's not very effective. In summer in Sydney the condensate from the air-conditioning in a small high rise building can supply enough water to make an artificial creek. In really dry inland places here in Australia evaporative cooling works really well but it's not always a good idea as way too much water can be lost to the atmosphere in some of the driest places.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you know I went to Alice springs for a couple of weeks two years ago and I went via Sydney - so yes I geography matters - cheers mate

replies.author: @Cooliemasteroz
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith like a lot of international travelers you’ve probably seen more of Australia than me.

author: @sasjadv
text:
Well, I think that if a device cools air by increasing humidity then it's a humidifier and certainly not an aircon. Cool air that's extra humid is worse than hot air, because you cannot control your body temp by sweating if it's to humid.
-unless it blows the humidified air outside, and uses a radiator or intercooler to cool another air-stream that's held indoors. In that case I can call it an air-conditioner.

author: @billbaggins
text:
Love the condition of your shirt Rob 👍 makes me feel so much better about my own work grots 😁
Thorts on heat pumps for the HVAC gurus.. heat pumps are quite efficient, fridges less so and hot water cylinders woefully wasteful. A few years ago, hot water heat pumps were a thing (not sure if they still are). Would it be feasible to combine house heating, hot water and beer cooling into a single unit?  ie a larger fridge unit that also heats water with a freezer unit that provides house heating and cooling. Would obviously need some rethinking of house design to incorporate a multi function plant room in a suitable location to service cooking and living areas. initial cost may be high but overall savings may be significant?
Beyond my knowledge to speculate too much but this seems such a good place to dump some of my random beer fuelled ideas 🤣🍺

author: @robertgaines-tulsa
text: I personally think to be classified as air conditioning, the air must be both cooled and dried. The original purpose for air conditioning was to condition the air for factory use. These usually involve some sort of heat pump that collects heat and dumps it somewhere else. Swamp coolers are just humidifiers that blow air over moistened filters. If you don't live in a dry region, they won't work very well.

replies.author: @robertgaines-tulsa
replies.text: I actually run one of those humidifiers on a humidistat to keep the air from getting too dry when the air conditioner is running. Yeah, it does put out cold air. The funny thing about air conditioners is that if the air conditioner is too small, it will get really dry, while if the air conditioner is too big, it won't be as effective at dehumidifying.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lots of dry regions mate - but I don't disagree with what you are saying equally I think it is best not to get too tied up with the minutia.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Very good.  Just a note that, while moisture in the air is useful for heating and evaporative cooling, drier air is better for cooling the local environment for people.  You might have some salt-contaminated filtering material laying about from water purification experiments -- running your output air across dried salt on one such filter might reduce the temperature a little more, while slowly cleaning the filter.  The more humidity is reduced, the cooler the air temperature will be.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I've been saving money to buy a used bus to convert it into an RV, and have been working on plans for the build in the meantime.  For heating and cooling, I was hoping to build a false floor and circulate fluid under it that I could either heat or cool.  This system would be a great compliment to that - simply divert the same fluid to pass through a radiator with an air pump as you've done.  It would be highly efficient, with the bulk of the energy needed just for heating or cooling the fluid, and very little energy spent on pumping the fluid and air.  😊

replies.author: @easy_s3351
replies.text: In the first video, which my initial purist comment was to, the unit consisted of a bellow that moved air. That's not "a system for controlling the humidity, ventilation, and temperature" but a system that only ventilates, basically nothing more than the fan at your bed side does (which you wouldn't call an AC, would you?) , hence my remark. Now that additional parts are added, like the heat exchanger in video 3, it is becoming an air conditioner because with it the unit will be able to (de)humidify, ventilate and cool/warm. :P
